title: Gut Path Template Showcase
description: Realistic structured-template review set for the gut path: psychotherapy, embodiment, emotional process, and repair.

slides.title: Repairing the Protective Loop
slides.subtitle: A section opener for emotional processing, inner contact, and embodied repair.
slides.body: We begin with the whole map: orient the body, track the protective move, make contact with the need, and practice one repair response.

body.text: A symptom is often a loyal protection that has outlived the moment it was built for.
The task is not to defeat it, but to understand what it has been carrying.
A client notices a sharp pull to withdraw whenever feedback arrives. The first layer is anger, but the body tells a more precise story: tight chest, lifted shoulders, and a held breath.
The slide should support a slow movement from visible reaction to vulnerable need without making the process feel clinical or abstract.
Good gut slides need warmth, sequence, and concrete language that a facilitator can read while staying relationally present.
Before explaining the pattern, pause and ask where it lives.
A sensation is not the whole truth, but it is often the first honest witness.

slides.title: A Room Safe Enough For Contact
slides.body: Use full-image slides when the emotional climate matters before the teaching point arrives.

slides.title: Finding The Unmet Need
slides.body: Split-image works well when the visual can carry the vulnerability while the text keeps the method clear.
bullets: Name the surface reaction without arguing with it.
Track the body signal that stays present.
Ask what the protected part needed then.
Offer a response small enough to receive.

slides.title: The Protective Part

body.text: A protective part is trying to prevent a familiar hurt from happening again.
This panel style should compete directly with the legacy slides: strong image, readable copy, and a grounded emotional atmosphere.

bullets: Respect the protection before inviting change.
Locate the body signal.
Offer contact, not correction.

slides.title: Four Moves In Chair Work
slides.body: Title-bullets should feel like a clean facilitator card: direct, warm, and easy to scan.
bullets: Set two clear positions.
Let each side speak in first person.
Reflect the emotion beneath the argument.
Look for the need both sides protect.

slides.title: Signals Worth Slowing Down For
slides.body: Use the basic bullet template for short lists that need emphasis without extra structure.
bullets: Sudden certainty.
A smile that arrives too early.
Explaining instead of feeling.
A phrase repeated with the same rhythm.
Relief after one accurate reflection.

slides.title: Repair Session Cheat Sheet
slides.body: Dense-bullets should hold a lot without becoming a wall of text.

slides.title: Reaction And Need
slides.subtitle: Separate what protects from what longs.

columns.title: Protective Reaction
columns.body: Pushes away, criticises, freezes, performs, or disappears to reduce risk.

columns.title: Primary Need
columns.body: Asks for safety, recognition, comfort, agency, or permission to exist.

columns.title: Facilitator Stance
columns.body: Validate the protection while listening for the need it cannot say directly.

slides.title: Five Minutes With A Felt Sense
slides.body: The steps template should make a live exercise feel followable.

steps.title: Orient
steps.text: Look around and choose one thing that tells the body this is now.

steps.title: Locate
steps.text: Find the strongest sensation connected to the issue.

steps.title: Describe
steps.text: Use texture, temperature, movement, or pressure.

steps.title: Ask
steps.text: What is this place afraid would happen if it softened?

steps.title: Receive
steps.text: Let one kind response land before moving on.

slides.title: Safety Check
slides.body: Checklist slides should be practical enough to use during facilitation.

items.title: Window
items.text: Affect is present but not flooding.

items.title: Choice
items.text: The client can pause, redirect, or decline.

items.title: Body
items.text: There is at least one grounding cue available.

items.title: Alliance
items.text: The facilitator is tracking consent, not just content.

slides.title: Is The Work Still Inside The Window?
The Inner Guard
slides.body: A profile slide can make a pattern feel specific without pathologising it.
bullets: Function: prevents humiliation.
Signal: heat in face and hands.
Need: dignity and choice.
Practice: speak one clean boundary.

slides.title: Repair In The Voice
slides.body: Media slides should introduce why the clip matters before attention moves to playback.

slides.title: The Protective Loop
slides.body: Timeline slides are useful when a pattern has predictable phases.

timeline.title: Trigger
timeline.body: Feedback touches an old expectation of rejection.

timeline.title: Shield
timeline.body: The system becomes sharp, busy, or unavailable.

timeline.title: Contact
timeline.body: The softer emotion becomes safe enough to name.

timeline.title: Need
timeline.body: A clear request or boundary emerges.

timeline.title: Repair
timeline.body: A new response is rehearsed while the body is still online.

slides.title: Signals And Interventions
rows: Affect is rising faster than contact
Slow the pace and reflect one phrase

slides.title: Repair Moves
slides.body: Card grids should make a family of interventions feel available at a glance.

cards.title: Empathic reflection
cards.body: Give the emotion words without taking it over.

cards.title: Two-chair dialogue
cards.body: Let conflicted parts become visible and differentiated.

cards.title: Imaginal repair
cards.body: Bring in the missing response while staying embodied.

cards.title: Boundary rehearsal
cards.body: Practice a sentence the nervous system can believe.

slides.title: Make The Next Move Small
slides.body: Choose one body signal and complete only the next ten seconds of contact: locate it, describe it, and ask what it is protecting.
slides.note: Stop while it still feels workable.
